attributes.stem:
A 68-year-old woman with stage V chronic kidney disease presents to the nephrology clinic for routine review. She has hypertension and type 2 diabetes. Her medications include irbesartan 300mg daily, amlodipine 10mg daily, metoprolol 50mg twice daily and linagliptin 5mg daily. She has not yet commenced dialysis.

On examination, she appears well. Her pulse rate is 72/min and blood pressure 165/85 mmHg. There is no peripheral oedema. Jugular venous pressure is not elevated. Heart sounds are dual with no murmurs. There are vesicular breath sounds throughout the chest on auscultation.

Recent blood tests show potassium 5.6 mmol/L (3.5-5.0), creatinine 420 μmol/L (45-90), and estimated glomerular filtration rate 8 mL/min/1.73m² (>90). ECG reveals nil changes suggestive of hyperkalaemia.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the MOST appropriate next step in management?
options: Discontinue irbesartan and commence prazosin 0.5mg twice daily
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with hyperkalaemia in the setting of advanced chronic kidney disease (CKD). The elevated potassium is likely due to the combination of reduced renal function and the use of irbesartan, an angiotensin receptor blocker (ARB) which can cause hyperkalaemia.

Addressing reversible causes of hyperkalaemia is always the first step in chronic management, which would include initiating a low-potassium diet and discontinuing contributing medications such as irbesartan. Discontinue irbesartan and commence prazosin 0.5mg twice daily is more of a correct answer than simply discontinue irbesartan. Given the patient's blood pressure is already 165/85 whilst taking irbesartan, it would likely be dangerously higher without it, hence starting an alternate agent at a modest dose (with view for uptitration as required) is a better approach.

Arranging urgent dialysis would be appropriate for refractory hyperkalaemia to medical management. It is hasty to start dialysis without addressing possible contributing factors first.

Commence furosemide 40mg daily and commence furosemide and spironolactone are both incorrect options, as the use of diuretics is inappropriate in this patient given they are euvolaemic. Whilst the former option may see a reduction in serum potassium levels, the latter option may not, given the potential of spironolactone to increase serum potassium levels.
learningPoints: Consideration should be given to discontinuing ACE inhibitors and ARBs in patients with chronic kidney disease who develop hyperkalaemia.
references: Goyal A, Cusick AS, Thielemier B. ACE Inhibitors. In: StatPearls [online]. 2023. Available at: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430896/
